cards.text: A {{c1::temporary object}} (or anonymous object) is an object created for a single expression that is {{c2::destroyed at the end of that expression}}.

To create a temporary class object:
{{c1::ClassName{arguments}}} or {{c2::ClassName(arguments)}}

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.code:
class Cents
{
public:
    Cents(int value) : m_value{value} { }
    int getValue() const { return m_value; }
private:
    int m_value;
};

std::cout << Cents{5}.getValue();
cards.answer: Prints 5. Creates a temporary Cents object with value 5, calls getValue(), then destroys the temporary.
cards.explanation: Cents{5} creates a temporary object that exists only for this expression. After getValue() returns, the temporary is destroyed.

cards.front: What is the lifetime of a temporary object?
cards.back: Temporary objects are created at the point of use and destroyed at the end of the full expression (typically at the semicolon). They don't persist beyond the statement that creates them.

cards.explanation:
Temporary objects are useful for:
  • Passing arguments to functions without creating named variables
  • Returning values from functions
  • Performing one-time operations without cluttering scope with named objects
  • Testing or debugging (quick object creation)
examples: return Cents{total};    // return temporary from function

cards.question: What does this function call do?
cards.answer: Creates a temporary Cents object with value 7, binds it to the const reference parameter, prints 7, then destroys the temporary after the function returns.
cards.explanation: Temporaries can be bound to const reference parameters, extending their lifetime for the duration of the function call.

cards.front: Can you bind a temporary object to a non-const reference?
cards.back: No. Temporary objects can only be bound to const lvalue references (const Type&) or rvalue references (Type&&). They cannot be bound to non-const lvalue references.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot bind a temporary to a non-const lvalue reference. Change parameter to const Cents& or Cents&&.
cards.explanation: Temporaries are rvalues and cannot be bound to non-const lvalue references. This prevents accidental modification of objects that will be immediately destroyed.

cards.text: When a temporary is bound to a {{c1::const reference}}, its lifetime is extended to match the {{c2::lifetime of the reference}}.

cards.front: When should you prefer named objects over temporary objects?
cards.back:
Use named objects when you need to:
  • Use the object multiple times
  • Debug and inspect the object's state
  • Make code more readable
Use temporaries for one-time use or when passing arguments.

cards.question: How many objects are created in this code?
cards.code:
Cents add(const Cents& c1, const Cents& c2)
{
    return Cents{c1.getValue() + c2.getValue()};
}

Cents result = add(Cents{5}, Cents{10});
cards.answer: Potentially 4 objects: two temporary parameters (5, 10), one temporary return value, and result. However, copy elision may reduce this.
cards.explanation: Temporaries are created for arguments and return value. Modern compilers often eliminate unnecessary copies through copy elision/RVO.
